title: Dog Ate a Small Box of Raisins: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Raisins are highly toxic to dogs and can cause kidney failure. Learn why this is a high-level emergency and what steps to take immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Raisin ingestion is a high-level medical emergency for dogs that can lead to rapid kidney failure. Contact a veterinarian immediately, as even a single raisin can be toxic and early treatment is critical.

What is raisin toxicity and how does it affect my dog?

Raisins are dried grapes, and both are known to be highly toxic to dogs. While the exact toxic agent is not fully understood, even a small amount can cause sudden kidney failure. Because raisins are concentrated versions of grapes, they are significantly more toxic by weight.

Is it an emergency if my dog eats a raisin?

  • Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is a medical emergency.
  • There is no known safe dose for raisins; some dogs may suffer severe poisoning from just one or two, while others may tolerate more.
  • Because we cannot predict an individual dog's sensitivity, you must contact a veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately.

How does taking a photo of the raisin packaging help the vet?

  • If you can, take a photo of the raisin box and any spilled contents to help the veterinary team estimate the total weight or volume consumed.
  • A photo of the packaging allows the vet to check for other harmful ingredients, such as xylitol or chocolate, which might be present in certain raisin-based snacks.

What are the immediate next steps if my dog ate raisins?

  • Do not wait for symptoms to appear before seeking professional help.
  • Seek early decontamination, such as induced vomiting or the administration of activated charcoal by a professional.
  • Early intervention is the most effective way to prevent permanent kidney damage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of raisins can cause acute renal failure in some dogs, with vomiting and/or diarrhea often developing within 6-12 hours. Other clinical signs include lethargy, anorexia, abdominal pain, weakness, dehydration, polydipsia, and tremors. Serum creatinine levels tend to rise early and disproportionately compared with serum urea nitrogen levels, and oliguric or anuric renal failure may develop within 24-72 hours of exposure. For asymptomatic dogs with recent ingestion of more than 1-2 g/kg, emesis should be induced, and activated charcoal may be beneficial with large ingestions. With large ingestions or in cases in which vomiting and/or diarrhea has spontaneously developed within 12 hours of ingestion, aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended, along with monitoring of renal function and fluid balance.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: How soon will my dog show symptoms of raisin poisoning?
faqs.answer: Symptoms like vomiting usually start within 6 to 12 hours of ingestion. Signs of kidney failure, such as lethargy or decreased urination, can take 24 to 72 hours to develop.

faqs.question: Can I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: You should never induce vomiting without first consulting a veterinarian. Doing so improperly can lead to aspiration pneumonia or other complications.

faqs.question: Is one raisin really that dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because the toxic reaction is idiosyncratic (varies widely between individuals), even a single raisin can cause acute renal failure in sensitive dogs.
